items.foco: Definition of regular and irregular astigmatism
items.title: Regular and irregular astigmatism
items.contentHtml:
Definition of regular and irregular astigmatism

  • Regular astigmatism: the main meridians are perpendicular to each other, generating a Sturm conoid with two linear foci; correctable with a spherocylindrical cylindrical lens.
  • Irregular astigmatism: the meridians are not perpendicular or uniform, frequently due to keratoconus, corneal scars or pellucid marginal degeneration; It is not fully correctable with conventional lenses and requires rigid contact lenses or surgery.
  • Corneal topography is essential to differentiate both types and plan treatment.

items.title: tear rule
items.contentHtml:
Cylinder axis: teardrop rule

  • The teardrop rule states that the axis of the corrective cylinder is oriented towards the palpebral fissure, similar to the shape of a teardrop.
  • In manual refraction, the cylinder axis is determined by rotating the Jackson cross cylinder until the patient sees the lines of the optotype equally sharp.
  • The rule helps to remember that the most curved meridian (and therefore the axis of the negative cylinder) usually aligns with the position of the eyelids during reading.
  • Trap: do not confuse axis with power; The axis is measured in degrees from 0° to 180°.

items.foco: Cornea as the main site of refraction
items.title: Cornea: main site of refraction
items.contentHtml:
Cornea as the main site of refraction

  • The cornea provides approximately 2/3 of the total refractive power of the eye (about 43 diopters), being the main site of refraction.
  • Corneal astigmatism is due to an aspherical curvature of the cornea; internal (lenticular) astigmatism contributes to a lesser extent.
  • Keratometry measures corneal curvature in the main meridians; a difference >1 D between meridians indicates significant astigmatism.
  • Corneal topography evaluates the entire surface, essential to detect keratoconus and irregular astigmatism.

items.foco: Myopia vs hyperopia in mixed astigmatism
items.title: Mixed astigmatism: myopia and hyperopia
items.contentHtml:
Myopia vs hyperopia in mixed astigmatism

  • In mixed astigmatism, one meridian is myopic (focus anterior to the retina) and the other is hyperopic (focus posterior to the retina).
  • Myopia in one meridian means that the refractive power is excessive; hyperopia in the other meridian indicates insufficient power.
  • The conoid of Sturm has a focus in front and another behind the retina; The circle of minimal confusion is located on the retina.
  • Clinically, the patient can see blurred both at distance and near.

items.foco: Classification: against the rule, for, oblique
items.title: Classification of astigmatism
items.contentHtml:
Classification: against the rule, in favor, oblique

  • Astigmatism in favor of the rule: the most curved meridian (greatest power) is close to the vertical (90° ± 15°); It is the most common in young people.
  • Astigmatism against the rule: the most curved meridian is close to the horizontal (180° ± 15°); common in the elderly and after cataract surgery.
  • Oblique astigmatism: the most curved meridian is between 30° and 60°, or between 120° and 150°.
  • The classification is relevant for choosing the type of intraocular lens and predicting changes with age.

items.foco: Symptoms: blurred vision, headache, eye fatigue
items.title: Symptoms of astigmatism
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms: blurred vision, headache, eye fatigue

  • Blurry vision both far and near, with distortion of images (shadow or lengthening effect).
  • Frontal headache and eye fatigue (asthenopia) due to constant accommodative effort, especially when reading or using screens.
  • Patients may squint their eyelids. (pinhole effect) to temporarily improve visual acuity.
  • In children, it can manifest as poor school performance or refusal to read.

items.title: Jackson Cross Cylinder
items.contentHtml:
Crossed Jackson Cylinder for Refinement

  • The Crossed Jackson Cylinder (CCJ) is a twin-cylinder lens of equal powers and opposite signs (for example +0.25 D and -0.25 D) with perpendicular axes.
  • It is used to refine the cylinder axis and cylinder power during refraction subjective.
  • For the axis: the CCJ is rotated and the patient is asked if vision improves when rotating the handle; The correct axis is the one where there is no difference.
  • For the power: the CCJ is flipped and the position that gives the best clarity is chosen; the final power is adjusted according to the response.

items.title: Keratometry and topography
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic corneal keratometry and topography

  • Keratometry measures corneal curvature in two main meridians (K1 and K2), reporting the radius of curvature in mm and the power in diopters.
  • Corneal astigmatism is calculated as the difference between K2 and K1 (most curved meridian minus plus plane).
  • Corneal topography provides a color map of the corneal surface, detecting irregularities, keratoconus and irregular astigmatism.
  • Presurgical indications: all refractive and cataract surgery requires topography to rule out corneal pathology.

items.title: Astigmatism treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: lenses, contact lenses, LASIK

  • Ophthalmic lenses: spherocylindrical lenses with negative cylinder; the axis of the cylinder is placed in degrees depending on the refraction.
  • Contact lenses: toric (soft) lenses or rigid gas permeable (RGP) contact lenses for irregular astigmatism.
  • LASIK and PRK are refractive surgeries that ablate the corneal stroma to regularize the curvature; indicated in stable regular astigmatism.
  • First line in simple astigmatism: glasses or contact lenses; refractive surgery when there are contraindications or patient preference.

items.foco: Keratoconus as a cause of irregular astigmatism
items.title: Keratoconus and irregular astigmatism
items.contentHtml:
Keratoconus as a cause of irregular astigmatism

  • Keratoconus is a bilateral corneal ectasia characterized by thinning and conical protrusion, generating irregular astigmatism.
  • Findings: Munson's sign (deformation of the lower eyelid in downward gaze), Fleischer's ring (deposition of iron in the epithelium), Vogt's striae (vertical lines in deep stroma).
  • Diagnosis: corneal topography with asymmetry pattern and increased central curvature (>47 D).
  • Treatment: RGP contact lenses in early stages; corneal cross-linking to stop progression; keratoplasty in advanced cases.

items.title: Post-cataract astigmatism
items.contentHtml:
Cataract surgery-induced astigmatism

  • Cataract surgery-induced astigmatism (SIA) depends on the incision: size, location, and suturing technique.
  • Small clear corneal incisions (<2.2 mm) induce less astigmatism (<0.5 D).
  • The rule of Javal estimates total astigmatism from the keratometric: Total astigmatism = Corneal astigmatism x 1.25 + (-0.50 x 90°) (classical formula).
  • To correct pre-existing astigmatism during surgery, toric intraocular lenses or limbal relaxing incisions are used.

items.title: Javal's rule
items.contentHtml:
Javal's rule for estimating astigmatism

  • Javal's rule is an empirical formula to estimate total refractive astigmatism from keratometric corneal astigmatism.
  • Formula: Total astigmatism = (corneal astigmatism × 1.25) + (lenticular cylinder of -0.50 D at 90°)..
  • The average lenticular astigmatism is assumed to be -0.50 D with axis at 90°.
  • Useful in clinical practice to verify subjective refraction; currently less used due to the precision of topography and aberrometry.
